title: Java Mutable Hash Keys
description: Practice why mutating a stored key strands it inside a hashed collection.
learningGoals: Explain why hashed collections look up entries by the key's current hashCode bucket.
Keep fields used by equals and hashCode immutable while an object is stored as a key.

completionChecks.explanation: The entry was bucketed under hash("urgent") when it was added; after the mutation both lookups probe the bucket for hash("later"), which never held the entry, so both print false.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens if urgent.name is set back to "urgent" before the two contains calls?
choices: Both calls print false because the set cached a stale hash at add time
The code prints false then true because the original bucket matches again
The code prints true then false because the first probe now finds the entry
Both calls print true because equals now matches in any bucket
completionChecks.explanation: HashSet keeps the entry in the bucket computed at add time; restoring the field restores that hashCode, so contains(urgent) finds it again while the probe for "later" still finds nothing.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens if tags.remove(urgent) is called immediately after urgent.name = "later"?
choices: It returns false and the set size remains 1
It returns true and the set size becomes 0
It throws ConcurrentModificationException because the key was mutated
It fails to compile because hashCode depends on a mutable field
completionChecks.explanation: The trap is expecting identity to rescue the removal; HashSet first probes the bucket for the current hash and misses the stored entry.
